desc: Medically significant — the only dangerous spider regularly encountered in LA County. Glossy black with red hourglass on ventral abdomen. Webs in dark, undisturbed areas.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅓ in–⅔ in body. ♂: much smaller.
fm.Web: Irregular, strong cobweb in sheltered dark areas
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes; poor vision; relies on vibrations
fm.Habitat: Under rocks, woodpiles, outdoor structures, garages
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; shy; bites only when trapped against skin
fm.Season: Year-round; males wander seeking mates in spring–fall

desc: Invasive — arrived in SoCal ~2000s, now abundant. Tan-brown with geometric pattern. Spiky egg sac diagnostic. Less venomous than Black Widow but rapidly displacing it.

fm.Web: Irregular cobweb under outdoor furniture, eaves, cars
fm.Habitat: Urban — under patio furniture, mailboxes, playground equipment
fm.Behavior: Less aggressive than Black Widow; frequently encountered

desc: Often confused with Black Widow. Dark with bulbous abdomen but lacks red hourglass. Essentially harmless.

fm.Web: Cobweb in dark corners, garages, basements
fm.Behavior: Shy; bite causes mild, brief pain only

desc: Largest and most conspicuous jumping spider in LA. Bright red abdomen contrasts with black cephalothorax. Active visual hunter with charismatic behavior.

fm.Web: No hunting web; silken retreat for resting/molting
fm.Habitat: Open sunny areas, fences, walls, garden structures
fm.Behavior: Active diurnal hunter; curious; turns to face observers

desc: Black with white spots and iridescent green chelicerae. One of the most commonly encountered jumping spiders.

fm.Eyes: 4 large anterior eyes; excellent binocular vision
fm.Habitat: Gardens, buildings, fences — any sunny surface
fm.Behavior: Active hunter; stalks prey with cat-like approach

desc: Introduced tropical species now abundant in urban LA. ♂ distinctive with white lateral stripes on black body.

fm.Habitat: Buildings, walls, window frames; strongly synanthropic
fm.Behavior: Active diurnal hunter; very fast

desc: Large, spectacular orb weaver. Female builds web up to 2 feet across with distinctive zigzag stabilimentum in center.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅔ in–1 in body (very large). ♂: ⅛ in–⅓ in.
fm.Web: Large circular orb with thick zigzag stabilimentum
fm.Habitat: Gardens, meadows, tall vegetation in sunny areas
fm.Behavior: Sits head-down in web center; bounces web to capture prey

desc: Silvery orb weaver with lobed abdomen. Web has stabilimentum. Common in warm SoCal gardens.

fm.Web: Orb web with X or cross-shaped stabilimentum
fm.Habitat: Gardens, warm dry areas, coastal scrub
fm.Behavior: Sits in web center with legs in X-shape

desc: The most common large orb weaver in LA gardens. Builds new web nightly in the same location. Variable coloring.

fm.Web: Vertical orb web; rebuilt nightly; sits in center at night
fm.Habitat: Gardens, under eaves, near outdoor lights (attracts prey)
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal web builder; hides during day; attracted to lights

desc: Large, fast spider frequently found indoors. Builds funnel-shaped sheet web. Males wander seeking females in fall.

fm.Size: ♀: ½ in–⅔ in body; leg span up to 3 in
fm.Web: Flat sheet web with funnel retreat in corners
fm.Habitat: Indoors — garages, basements, closets, window wells
fm.Behavior: Fast runner when disturbed; males wander in fall

desc: Large, flat-bodied spider with legs spread crab-like. Nocturnal ambush predator on walls and ceilings. Harmless.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅔ in–¾ in body; very large leg span
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, rocky areas; sometimes enters homes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flat against surfaces; extremely fast

desc: One of the largest wolf spiders. Females carry egg sac on spinnerets, then carry spiderlings on back. Ground-dwelling nocturnal hunter.

fm.Web: No web; digs shallow burrow or roams
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes; 2 large posterior median eyes reflective at night
fm.Habitat: Open ground, gardens, under debris; burrows in soil
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; active hunter; ♀ carries egg sac then spiderlings on back

desc: Ambush predator that changes color between white and yellow to match flower color. Catches pollinators on blooms.

fm.Web: No web; ambush predator on flowers
fm.Habitat: On flowers — especially composites, roses, buckwheat
fm.Behavior: Color-changing ambush predator; catches bees and butterflies on flowers

desc: The "daddy long-legs" of buildings. Extremely common indoors. Vibrates web rapidly when disturbed.

fm.Size: ♀: ¼ in–⅓ in body; very long legs (1–2 in)
fm.Web: Loose irregular web in corners, ceilings, garages
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes in two groups of three + two
fm.Behavior: Vibrates rapidly when disturbed ("vibrating spider"); preys on other spiders

desc: Introduced. Brick-red cephalothorax and large forward-pointing fangs specialized for piercing isopod (sowbug) armor. Found under logs and bark.

fm.Web: Silken retreat under bark/logs; no prey web
fm.Habitat: Under bark, logs, rocks, leaf litter — wherever sowbugs live
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal sowbug specialist; large fangs pierce isopod armor

desc: LA County's native tarantula. Males wander in fall seeking mates — the famous "tarantula migration." Docile, long-lived (♀ 20+ years). Burrow dweller.

fm.Size: ♀: body 1.5–2 in; leg span up to 5 in
fm.Web: Lines burrow with silk; no prey web
fm.Eyes: 8 tiny eyes; poor vision; relies on vibration
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, grasslands, oak woodland; hillside burrows
fm.Behavior: ♂ wander Sep–Nov seeking ♀; docile; flicks urticating hairs if harassed

desc: Native to SoCal deserts — the only recluse spider established in the LA region. Medically significant bite (necrotic). Violin marking on cephalothorax; 6 eyes (not 8).

fm.Web: Irregular sheet web in dark recesses
fm.Eyes: 6 eyes in 3 pairs (diagnostic — most spiders have 8)
fm.Habitat: Desert and semi-arid areas; rock crevices; occasionally buildings
fm.Behavior: Extremely shy; nocturnal; bites only when pressed against skin

desc: Native SoCal trapdoor spider. Builds silk-lined burrow with hinged lid camouflaged with debris. Ambush predator.

fm.Web: Silk-lined burrow with hinged trapdoor lid
fm.Behavior: Sits in burrow, lid slightly open; lunges at passing prey

desc: The largest scorpion in North America — up to 6 in. Desert-edge LA County. Fluoresces brilliant green under UV light. Sting painful but not medically significant.

fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, sandy washes — northeast LA County
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; ambush predator; fluoresces green under UV

desc: The most commonly encountered scorpion in residential SoCal. Small, tan, found under rocks and in homes. Mild sting.

fm.Habitat: Under rocks, bark, debris; enters homes; gardens
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides in moist dark areas; UV-detectable

desc: Green lynx spider — bright green ambush predator on flowers. Female guards egg sac aggressively and can spit venom up to 20cm. Important natural enemy of crop pests. Common on garden flowers.

fm.Behavior: Ambush; ♀ guards egg sac; spits venom

desc: European garden spider — large orb weaver with distinctive white cross pattern on abdomen. Introduced from Europe. Builds large orb webs in gardens. Common in fall.

fm.Color: Brown-orange with white cross on abdomen

desc: Banded garden spider — large orb weaver with silver and dark zigzag banding. Builds large orb webs in tall grass and gardens. Stabilimentum (zigzag silk) in web center.

fm.Color: Silver with dark bands; zigzag stabilimentum

desc: Yellow sac spider — pale yellow-green spider building silk retreats in curled leaves and corners. Nocturnal wandering hunter. Enters homes frequently. Mild bite causes temporary redness — one of the most commonly encountered indoor spiders.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides in sac by day

desc: Western parson spider — dark fast-running ground spider with white dorsal stripe ('collar'). Nocturnal hunter under rocks and debris. Can bite but not dangerous.

fm.Color: Dark with white dorsal collar mark; very fast

desc: Marbled cellar spider — long-legged spider with marbled abdomen. Introduced from Mediterranean. Builds messy webs in garages and building overhangs. Displacing native cellar spiders.

fm.Size: ⅓ in body; very long legs

desc: Introduced. Similar to Desert Recluse. Occasionally found in imported goods/structures. 6 eyes.

fm.Web: Irregular sheet web in dark recesses
fm.Behavior: Very shy; nocturnal; medically significant bite (rare)

desc: Noble false widow — introduced from Canary Islands, now spreading aggressively in SoCal. Dark with cream crescent marking. Bite can cause localized pain and inflammation. Displacing native species.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; bite mildly medically significant

desc: Gray wall jumper — flattened jumping spider of sun-warmed walls and buildings. Two prominent white side stripes on dark body. Pantropical species common in urban LA. Very approachable.

fm.Behavior: Hunts on warm walls; flattened for crack-hiding

desc: Spotted orbweaver — large rusty-brown orb weaver with cruciate (cross-shaped) dark mark on abdomen. Builds enormous webs on buildings at night. Very common urban spider.

fm.Color: Rusty brown with dark cross-mark on abdomen

desc: California red jumping spider — large bold jumper with bright red abdomen and black markings. Males display in elaborate courtship. On chaparral shrubs. CA near-endemic.

fm.Behavior: Active hunter; curious; red color distinctive

desc: Pacific Coast tick — the most commonly encountered tick on LA hiking trails. Three-host tick that feeds on lizards as larvae, rodents as nymphs, and large mammals as adults. Can transmit Rocky Mountain spotted fever and tularemia.

fm.Size: ⅛ in unfed; ½ in engorged
fm.Behavior: Quests on grass tips for passing hosts

desc: Grape jumping spider — tiny colorful jumper found on grape and other plants. ♂ with bright red-orange face and metallic green chelicerae. Elaborate courtship display.

fm.Color: ♂ red face, green chelicerae; ♀ brown striped

desc: Fire-faced paradise spider — strikingly colorful jumper with red face and green chelicerae in males. Elaborate courtship dance includes leg-waving, body vibrations, and color displays. Ground-dwelling.

fm.Behavior: ♂ elaborate courtship dance with colors; studied for vision

desc: California common scorpion — the most frequently encountered scorpion in LA County. Small tan scorpion with mild sting. Fluoresces bright green under UV light. Nocturnal — common under rocks.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; glows under UV; mild sting

desc: Steindachner's ebony tarantula — the most commonly encountered tarantula in LA County. Males cross roads and trails in fall seeking mates. Dark brown-black. Docile — rarely bites.

fm.Behavior: ♂ wander roads Oct–Nov; docile; urticating hairs

desc: Western black-legged tick — primary Lyme disease vector on the West Coast. Smaller and darker than the Pacific Coast tick. Quests on vegetation for passing hosts. Peak activity in winter/spring.

fm.Behavior: Transmits Lyme disease; nymphs highest risk spring

desc: Simon's hacklemesh spider — introduced South American spider now very common in SoCal. Builds fuzzy hackled-band webs on fences and walls. Fast runner. Dark brown. Often mistaken for brown recluse.

fm.Behavior: Very fast; fuzzy bluish web; harmless

desc: Pocoki's burrowing scorpion — large thick-tailed scorpion excavating deep burrows. Ambushes prey from burrow entrance. Thick metasoma (tail) diagnostic. Mild sting.
Grey house spider — introduced Australian spider building distinctive lace-sheet webs on window frames, fences, and walls. Very common on building exteriors. Expanding in SoCal since ~1990s.

fm.Behavior: Builds lace-web; very common on buildings

desc: Western colonus — small dark jumping spider extremely common on buildings, fences, and garden walls. Active day-hunter with excellent vision. One of the most frequently seen spiders in urban LA.

fm.Behavior: Active hunter; common urban jumper

desc: White-banded crab spider — remarkable color-changing ambush spider on flowers. Can slowly shift between white and yellow over days to match the flower it hunts on. Grabs pollinators visiting flowers.

fm.Behavior: Changes color white↔yellow to match flower; ambush predator

desc: Pacific broad-faced sac spider — dark reddish-brown spider with notably wide head. Nocturnal hunter under bark and debris. Can bite but not medically significant.

fm.Habitat: Under bark, leaf litter, enters homes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; broad cephalothorax distinctive

desc: Extraordinary hunting method — swings sticky bolas ball on silk thread to catch moths. Lures with moth pheromone mimicry.

fm.Web: No orb — swings single sticky silk bolas
fm.Habitat: Shrubs, gardens — where moths fly at night
fm.Behavior: Swings sticky silk ball at moths; mimics moth pheromones; unique hunting strategy

desc: The only scorpion in NA with potentially life-threatening sting. Climbs walls. Rare in LA County — desert edge.

fm.Habitat: Desert — under bark, in rock crevices; climbs walls/ceilings
fm.Behavior: Climbs vertical surfaces; bark-loving; most medically significant NA scorpion

desc: Bright orange-red jumping spider. Males with striking display. In chaparral and scrub.
Elongated spider with huge chelicerae. Builds orb webs near water. Males and females lock jaws during mating.

fm.Body: Elongated spider with huge chelicerae

desc: Triangulate cobweb spider — small house spider with distinctive zigzag triangular marks on abdomen. Extremely common indoors. Preys on other spiders including brown widows. Beneficial.
Spined micrathena — bizarre-looking spider with sharp spines projecting from a triangular abdomen. Small orb web in woodland understory. Despite fierce appearance, completely harmless.

fm.Body: Bizarre spider with abdominal spines

desc: Green spider that hunts on vegetation. Does not build web. Ambushes prey with excellent vision.

fm.Body: Green spider that hunts on vegetation

desc: Desert blonde tarantula — golden-haired tarantula of desert margins. Males wander widely in fall seeking mates, often crossing roads. At edge of range in LA County. Docile.
Builds sheet webs with funnel retreat in grass and low vegetation. Very fast. Venom studied for neuroscience.

fm.Web: Funnel sheet web in grass, shrubs
fm.Behavior: Extremely fast — venom studied in neuroscience

desc: Slow spider that spits sticky venomous silk to immobilize prey from a distance. Six eyes.
Whip scorpion that sprays acetic acid (vinegar). Nocturnal desert predator. Harmless to humans.

fm.Venom: None — whip scorpion, not true scorpion

desc: Largest jumping spider in NA. Black with white spots and iridescent chelicerae. Popular in pet trade.

fm.Color: Black with white spots; iridescent green chelicerae

desc: Variable color orbweaver — yellow, orange, or marbled. Round abdomen. Fall webbuilder.

fm.Body: Variable color orbweaver — yellow, orange, or marbled

desc: Southern house spider — large dark spider in old buildings. ♂ slender with long palps; ♀ stocky. Males often mistaken for brown recluse. Builds messy webs in crevices. Harmless.

fm.Color: ♀ dark brown stocky; ♂ tan slender with long palps

desc: Long-legged spider in garages and cellars. Native species (unlike the European P. phalangioides).

fm.Web: Irregular web in cellars, dark spaces
fm.Behavior: Long-legged cellar spider

desc: Tiny black and white striped jumper on walls and windows. European introduction. Hunts flies.

fm.Behavior: Stalks flies on vertical surfaces

desc: Orbweaver with distinctive horn-like bumps on abdomen. Builds large webs on porches in fall.

fm.Color: Brown/gray with horn-like bumps on abdomen
fm.Web: Large orb web on porches, eaves

desc: Orange-red abdomen with black cephalothorax. Complex courtship dance. Found in meadows and gardens.
Two distinct male forms — tufted (black) and gray. Rare example of within-species dimorphism in spiders.

fm.Body: Two distinct male forms — tufted (black) and gray

desc: Translucent green jumping spider on leaves. Huge forward-facing eyes. Hunts small insects.

fm.Body: Translucent green jumping spider on leaves

desc: Small bronze-brown jumping spider on tree trunks and fences. White lateral bands. Very common.

fm.Body: Small bronze-brown jumping spider on tree trunks and fences

desc: Large orbweaver with round spotted abdomen. Variable color — white, tan, or pink. Fall webbuilder.

fm.Body: Large orbweaver with round spotted abdomen

desc: Small orbweaver with unique star-shaped spiny abdomen. Nocturnal webbuilder on shrubs.

fm.Body: Small orbweaver with unique star-shaped spiny abdomen

desc: Pale yellow-green sac spider. Builds silk retreats in corners. Occasional biter — mild venom. European.
Small dark wolf spider abundant on ground in many habitats. Females carry egg sac and spiderlings.

fm.Behavior: ♀ carries egg sac on spinnerets

desc: Long-legged harvestman (daddy longlegs). Not a spider — single body segment, no venom, no silk.
Large tan solifuge of desert margins. Huge chelicerae. Nocturnal predator. Not venomous. Very fast.

fm.Habitat: Large tan solifuge of desert margins

desc: Tiny arachnid (<⅛ in) with scorpion-like pincers but no tail/stinger. Beneficial — eats mites, booklice.

fm.Behavior: Beneficial — eats booklice, dust mites

desc: Small dark cobweb spider. Common in buildings. European introduction. Harmless relative of widows.
Small orbweaver building incomplete web with open sector leading to retreat. On buildings year-round.

fm.Web: Incomplete orb with open sector + signal thread
fm.Behavior: Signal line leads to retreat — detects prey vibrations

desc: Tiny cribellate orbweaver. One of only two spider families worldwide that lack venom glands entirely.

fm.Behavior: No venom glands — wraps prey in silk only

desc: Dark ground spider of sac spider family. Found under rocks and debris in coastal sage scrub. Native to Baja and SoCal.

fm.Habitat: Under rocks, debris in coastal sage scrub

desc: Bright metallic green jumping spider with orange markings. Large for a jumper. On shrubs and low vegetation.

fm.Body: Bright metallic green jumping spider with orange markings

desc: Small pale jumping spider with white chelicerae. Common on bushes and flowers. Hunts small flies.

fm.Body: Small pale jumping spider with white chelicerae

desc: Iridescent green-bronze jumping spider. Ant-mimicking body shape. Found on low vegetation.

fm.Body: Iridescent green-bronze jumping spider

desc: Harmful ant-mimic jumper — elongated jumping spider that mimics ants in body shape and movement. Lives near ant trails. Despite common name, actually preys on ants. Fascinating Batesian mimicry.

fm.Body: Elongate ant-mimicking jumping spider

desc: Black ground spider with white spots mimicking velvet ants. Nocturnal hunter under rocks.

fm.Body: Black ground spider with white spots mimicking velvet ants

desc: Mouse spider. Velvety gray-brown. Nocturnal hunter in houses. European introduction.
Tiny spider building distinctive bowl-shaped sheet web with flat doily beneath. In shrubs and gardens.
Small dome-web spider in shaded understory. Inverted under dome, catches insects falling onto web.

fm.Body: Small dome-web spider in shaded understory

desc: Small orbweaver with distinctive lattice-like abdominal pattern. Nocturnal webbuilder on fences and plants.

fm.Body: Small orbweaver with distinctive lattice-like abdominal pattern

desc: Large velvety red mite emerging after rain. Predatory on insect eggs. Beneficial garden inhabitant.
Tiny red-brown mite invading homes in spring. Feeds on clover and grass. Leaves red stains when crushed.
Brown bark-matching crab spider. Ambushes prey on tree trunks. Excellent camouflage on oak bark.

fm.Body: Brown bark-matching crab spider

desc: Dark brown crab spider active on ground and leaf litter. Ambushes prey. Common in natural areas.

fm.Body: Dark brown crab spider active on ground and leaf litter

desc: Very common cobweb spider in homes worldwide. Brown with mottled pattern. Beneficial pest controller.
California trapdoor spider relative. Builds silk-lined burrow without door. Large, dark. Endemic to California.

fm.Behavior: Long-lived mygalomorph; does not build trapdoor

desc: Primitive mygalomorph building turret-shaped collar around burrow entrance. Long-lived. California native.

fm.Behavior: Primitive mygalomorph — builds turret not trapdoor

desc: Builds sheet web with funnel retreat in shrubs. Very fast — darts out to grab prey on web surface.

fm.Body: Builds sheet web with funnel retreat in shrubs

desc: Dark spider mimicking carpenter ants. Waves front legs like antennae. Ground-dwelling predator.

fm.Body: Dark spider mimicking carpenter ants

desc: Dark spider building cribellate webs under bark and in crevices. Blue-white hackled silk.

fm.Body: Dark spider building cribellate webs under bark and in crevices

desc: Enters other spiders webs, plucks silk to lure prey. Specialist spider predator.

fm.Body: Enters other spiders webs, plucks silk to lure prey

desc: Medium brown spider endemic to CA. Often confused with brown recluse but harmless.

fm.Body: Medium brown spider endemic to CA

desc: Flat fast spider hunting on ground and bark. Long front legs. Common in natural areas.

fm.Body: Flat fast spider hunting on ground and bark

desc: Elongate dark spider mimicking large black ants. Waves front legs like antennae. Ground-dwelling.

fm.Body: Elongate dark spider mimicking large black ants

desc: Tiny dome-web spider of forest understory. Male fights on female's web. Important prey for birds.

fm.Behavior: Males fight on female webs

desc: Dark ground spider with white dorsal stripe (parson's cravat). Fast nocturnal hunter. Occasional biter.

fm.Body: Dark ground spider with white dorsal stripe (parson's cravat)

desc: Tiny dark jumping spider with two white spots on pedipalps waved like flags during courtship.

fm.Body: Tiny dark jumping spider with two white spots on pedipalps waved like flags during courtship

desc: Small orbweaver of coastal scrub and garden hedges. Variable brown-gray coloring.
Large orbweaver found in warmer areas. Hides in curled leaf during day, builds large web at night.
Sand-colored wolf spider camouflaged on beaches. Nocturnal hunter of beach invertebrates.
Pale green lynx spider hunting on shrubs. Ambushes prey with excellent vision and quick strike.
Tiny spider that decorates its web with a vertical line of debris and dead prey — 'trash line.' Spider hides in the line, perfectly camouflaged.

fm.Color: Brown; humped abdomen; sits in debris line

desc: Slender spider on vegetation. Active hunter — pounces on prey from leaves. Prominent leg spines. Important predator of crop pests.

fm.Color: Pale with dark stripes; prominent leg spines

desc: Small dark wolf spider extremely common in gardens. Runs on ground hunting prey. Mothers carry egg sacs, then spiderlings ride on back.
Harvestman with spiny body. Not a spider — single body section, no silk, no venom. Common under rocks and in leaf litter in mountains.

fm.Color: Brown; spiny body; very long legs

desc: Tiny spider building distinctive bowl-shaped web above a flat doily-shaped sheet. Very common in shrubs. Web architecture is diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark with pale marks; distinctive bowl+doily web

desc: Large spider resembling wolf spider. Female carries egg sac in chelicerae (not on spinnerets). Builds nursery web tent for spiderlings.
Large flat fast spider. Not native — pantropical. Occasionally found in LA buildings. Harmless. Feared due to size but beneficial predator.
Medium brown spider recently established in California. Originally Mediterranean. Builds sheet webs. Can bite — mildly painful.

fm.Color: Brown with distinctive fiddle-like dorsal mark

desc: Bizarre spider that holds a small web between front legs and throws it over prey like a net. Enormous eyes for night hunting. Incredible behavior.

fm.Behavior: Holds expandable web between legs — throws it at prey

desc: Fast-moving solifuge (wind scorpion/camel spider). Not a spider — separate arachnid order. No venom. Enormous chelicerae. Nocturnal desert predator.

fm.Note: Not a spider — no venom, no silk

desc: Mule fat blister mite — tiny eriophyid mite causing conspicuous blister galls on mule fat (Baccharis) leaves. Galls are green-red raised bumps. Very common in riparian areas.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are raised blisters on mule fat leaves

desc: Koch's wolf spider — large ground-running wolf spider of open habitats. Nocturnal. Eyes reflect bright green with headlamp. Females carry egg sacs and young on their backs.

fm.Behavior: Eyes glow green with headlamp at night

desc: Ribbon jumping spider — dark jumping spider with distinctive white lateral stripes. Very common on tree bark and fences. Hunts actively by day. Often found on building exteriors.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with white lateral stripes

desc: Puritan scorpion — medium brown scorpion common in foothill and desert habitats. Found under rocks and bark. Mildly venomous — sting comparable to bee sting. Glows under UV light.

fm.Color: Brown; fluoresces blue-green under UV light
fm.Warning: Mild sting — comparable to bee sting

desc: Ghost spider — pale fast-running spider active at night. Builds no web — hunts by running down prey on foliage. Often found on walls at night near porch lights.
Trashline orbweaver — tiny spider decorating its web with a vertical line of debris. Spider sits motionless in the debris line, invisible to both predators and prey.

fm.Color: Brown; humped abdomen; hides in debris line

desc: Pacific orbweaver — medium brown orb weaver building webs in coastal scrub and chaparral. Nocturnal — takes down web at dawn. Common but secretive.

fm.Color: Brown with subtle markings; nocturnal web builder

desc: Jewel spider — large orb weaver with gem-like tubercles on abdomen. Builds enormous webs on buildings at night. Very common in suburban LA. Harmless.

cn: California Black Walnut Pouch Gall Mite
desc: Willow gall mite — microscopic eriophyid mite inducing conspicuous raised bead-like galls on willow leaves. Very common along riparian corridors. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are raised beads on willow leaves

desc: Sagebrush gall mite — microscopic eriophyid mite causing enlarged distorted buds on sagebrush (Artemisia). Common in coastal sage scrub. Galls are swollen fuzzy buds.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are swollen buds on sagebrush

desc: Foothill scorpion — medium brown scorpion of LA County foothills and canyons. Found under rocks and bark. Fluorescent blue-green under UV light. Mild sting.
Large spotted orbweaver — builds huge orb webs at night, takes them down by dawn. Hides in curled leaf during day. Distinctly spotted abdomen. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Brown with pale spots; nocturnal web builder

desc: Gertsch's sawfinger scorpion — small scorpion with distinctively serrated pedipalp fingers. Desert and foothill habitats. Nocturnal. Found under rocks. Mild sting.
Wall spider — tiny spider building small flat star-shaped webs on walls and ceilings. Extremely common on building exteriors worldwide. Often near light fixtures where it catches tiny insects.

fm.Color: Pale gray; flat star-shaped web on walls

desc: Superstition Mountains scorpion — dark robust scorpion. Found in rocky foothill habitats. Nocturnal. One of the larger scorpions in LA County mountains.
Catalina Island pseudoscorpion — tiny predatory arachnid resembling a scorpion without a tail. Lives in leaf litter. Grasps prey with pincers. Beneficial predator of mites and springtails.

fm.Color: Red-brown; scorpion-like pincers but no tail

desc: Graceful ground spider — small fast-running spider recently established in SoCal. Pantropical introduction. Found under debris in gardens. Builds no web.
Los Angeles scorpion — small-medium scorpion found in foothill canyons of the San Gabriel and Santa Monica Mountains. Nocturnal. Under rocks. Glows under UV.
American dog tick — medium-sized tick; major vector of Rocky Mountain spotted fever. Found in grassy and shrubby areas. Attaches to dogs, humans, and wildlife.

fm.Size: ⅛ in unfed; ½ in engorged
fm.Warning: Disease vector — check for ticks after hiking

desc: Pacific Coast tick — common tick of chaparral and grassland. Primary vector of 364D rickettsiosis in California. Attaches to deer, dogs, and humans.

fm.Color: Brown with ornate pale dorsal markings

desc: Minute jumping spider — extremely small jumper. Among the tiniest jumping spiders in LA County. On vegetation. Active hunter despite microscopic size.
California trapdoor spider — large primitive spider building silk-lined burrows with trapdoor. Massive chelicerae. Nocturnal. Foothill and mountain habitats. California endemic.
California paradise spider — tiny jumping spider — males perform elaborate courtship dances with colorful displays. Complex choreography studied by behavioral biologists.

fm.Color: ♂ colorful face+legs for courtship; ♀ cryptic brown

desc: McCook's wolf spider — medium wolf spider with tufted front legs used in courtship signaling. Males drum and wave leg tufts to females. Common in leaf litter.

fm.Color: Brown with pale median stripe; ♂ tufted front legs

desc: Palp-waving jumping spider — small gray jumping spider on building walls. Males wave enlarged palps during courtship. Very common on exterior walls and fences.

fm.Color: Gray with white markings; on walls

desc: Bordered ant-mimic spider — small ground spider that remarkably mimics ants in body shape and movement. Walks with front legs raised like antennae. On ground among ants.

fm.Color: Dark; ant-shaped body; mimics ant movement

desc: Willow eriophyid mite — microscopic mite causing curled and distorted willow leaves. Very common on willows along streams. Galls are diagnostic.
Long-palped lynx spider — bright green lynx spider similar to P. viridans but with longer palps. Ambush predator on flowers. Mothers guard egg sacs.

fm.Color: Bright green; spiny legs; long palps

desc: Gray lynx spider — small gray lynx spider on vegetation. Active predator. Prominent leg spines. Less common than striped and green lynx spiders.
Six-spotted orbweaver — tiny bright green orb weaver with six black spots on underside. Builds small orbs in shrubs. Very common but easily overlooked.
Arabesque orbweaver — medium orb weaver with intricate abdominal pattern. Builds web at dusk, removes at dawn. Common in gardens and woodland edges.
Athena's ant-mimic — dark ground spider remarkably resembling a large carpenter ant. Runs with ant-like gait. Predatory. Found among ants.
Dewdrop spider — tiny silver spider living as kleptoparasite in larger spiders' webs. Steals prey from host's web. Globular silver abdomen reflects light.
Poison oak leaf mite — microscopic eriophyid mite causing red blister galls on poison oak leaves. Very common wherever poison oak grows. Galls are raised red bumps.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; red blister galls on poison oak leaves

desc: Mann's jumping spider — small tan jumping spider common in chaparral vegetation. Active day-hunting predator with excellent vision. Males perform courtship displays.
Harford's jumping spider — small dark jumper of chaparral and sage scrub. Active day hunter on vegetation. Males with iridescent scales.
Thevenet's trapdoor spider — large dark trapdoor spider building silk-lined burrows with hinged door. California endemic. Nocturnal — waits with door slightly ajar to ambush prey.
Intertidal jumping spider — tiny spider living in splash zone of rocky shores. One of the few spiders adapted to marine intertidal habitat. Feeds on sea slaters and beach flies.

fm.Habitat: Rocky intertidal splash zone — unusual for a spider

desc: Oregon paradise spider — tiny jumping spider with elaborate male courtship. Males display colorful face and leg ornaments with complex choreography. Studied extensively.
Hairy tufted jumping spider — medium jumper with dense body hair. Hunts actively on vegetation and bark. Common in chaparral and woodland.
Aloe gall mite — microscopic mite inducing abnormal growth (galls) on aloe plants. Common on ornamental aloes in LA gardens. Causes warty/distorted growth.

fm.Color: Invisible; warty galls on aloe plants

desc: Schlingeri's trapdoor spider — large dark trapdoor spider building silk-lined burrows. California endemic. Nocturnal — ambushes prey from burrow entrance.
Kristena's trapdoor spider — trapdoor spider of foothill habitats. California endemic. Complex courtship — males drum on female's trapdoor to announce presence.
Vivid crab spider — bright green crab spider on flowers and foliage. Ambush predator. Can change shade slightly to match background. Common in gardens.
California jumping spider — medium jumping spider with bright green chelicerae. Active day-hunter. Common on fences, walls, and vegetation. Bold.
Small wolf spider — medium burrowing wolf spider of open sandy habitats. Nocturnal. Eyes reflect green with headlamp. Females carry egg sacs.

fm.Color: Brown; in burrows in sandy soil

desc: Live oak erineum mite — microscopic mite causing fuzzy erineum (felt-like growth) on live oak leaf undersides. Very common. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Invisible; fuzzy felt patches on oak leaf undersides

desc: Dawn wolf spider — large burrowing wolf spider active at dawn and dusk. Sits at burrow entrance waiting for prey. Eyes glow bright green with headlamp at night.

fm.Color: Brown; large; eyes glow green at night

desc: Elegant paradise spider — tiny jumping spider with ornate ♂ courtship display. Males wave colorful legs and palps in complex choreography. On ground in open habitats.
Butterfly orbweaver — medium orb weaver with wing-shaped abdominal projections resembling a butterfly. Unusual angular shape. Builds orb webs in shrubs.
Ragweed gall mite — microscopic eriophyid inducing enlarged distorted buds on ragweed. Very common wherever ragweed grows. Galls are diagnostic swollen buds.

fm.Color: Invisible; swollen bud galls on ragweed

desc: Thirsty jumping spider — small brown-tan jumper of dry open habitats. Active day-hunter with excellent vision. On ground and low vegetation.
Singular harvestman — long-legged harvestman (daddy longlegs) of oak woodland. Not a spider — no venom, no silk. Scavenges small invertebrates and plant material.
Chamise blister gall mite — microscopic mite causing raised blister galls on chamise (Adenostoma) leaves. Very common in chaparral — chamise is the dominant shrub.

fm.Color: Invisible; blister galls on chamise leaves

desc: Hairy paradise spider — tiny furry jumping spider with complex male courtship dance involving colorful displays. On ground in chaparral. Part of the speciose Habronattus radiation.
Yellow devil scorpion — medium yellowish scorpion of foothill and desert habitats. Mildly venomous — sting like a bee. Under rocks. Fluoresces under UV light.
Beck's desert scorpion — medium pale scorpion of desert and sandy habitats. Nocturnal. Glows blue-green under UV light. Mild sting — not medically significant.
Utah crab spider — brown bark-colored crab spider ambushing prey on tree trunks. Flat body pressed against bark. Common on oaks and pines.

fm.Color: Brown bark-pattern; flat against tree trunk

desc: Dorsate dictynid — small spider building irregular meshwork webs on vegetation. Common in chaparral. Part of the diverse meshweb spider family.

fm.Color: Brown; small meshwork web on plants

desc: California flattened jumping spider — flat-bodied salticid living on tree bark and walls. Flattened shape adapted for life in narrow crevices. Hunts by day on sun-warmed surfaces.
Nike jumping spider — medium bold jumping spider. Active diurnal hunter on vegetation and fences. Excellent binocular vision. Curious and approachable.
Common house combfoot — tiny cobweb spider common in buildings. Builds small tangle webs in corners. Variable coloring. One of many small theridiids indoors.
Live oak erineum mite — microscopic mite causing fuzzy felt patches (erinia) on coast live oak leaf undersides. Very common. Felt patches turn brown with age.

fm.Color: Invisible; fuzzy felt patches on oak leaves

desc: Proszynski's jumping spider — small salticid named for the renowned spider taxonomist Jerzy Proszynski. Active day-hunter on vegetation and buildings.
Northern orbweaver — medium orb-weaving spider building webs in shrubs and low vegetation. Distinctive leaf-shaped abdominal pattern.
Coronado harvestman — small short-legged harvestman of moist habitats. Unlike typical daddy longlegs, has shorter legs. Under rocks and in leaf litter in canyons.

fm.Color: Brown; short legs for a harvestman

desc: Morose jumping spider — tiny dark jumper. On ground in leaf litter and under rocks. Part of the diverse salticid fauna. Active day-hunter despite small size.
Baccharis leaf blister mite — microscopic mite causing blister galls on coyote brush (Baccharis) leaves. Raised bumps on leaves diagnostic. Common in sage scrub.

fm.Color: Invisible; blister galls on Baccharis leaves

desc: Half-edged wall jumping spider — flattened pale jumper on building walls. Originally Mediterranean, now established in SoCal. Hunts small insects on sun-warmed surfaces.
Thalia ant-mimic spider — ground spider mimicking ants in shape and movement. Walks with front pair of legs raised like antennae. Predatory — hunts among ants.
Diminutive jumping spider — extremely tiny salticid. Among the smallest jumping spiders in LA County. On ground and leaf litter. Active hunter despite microscopic size.
Schlinger's paradise spider — tiny jumping spider with elaborate ♂ courtship involving colorful displays and complex choreography. Named for Schlinger. Ground-dwelling.
Striped spitting spider — small spider that spits a mixture of silk and venom onto prey from a distance. Slow-moving nocturnal predator. Striped legs distinctive.

fm.Behavior: Spits silk+venom at prey from distance

desc: Detrimental orbweaver — medium orb weaver of woodland and garden edges. Builds web at dusk. Variable patterning. Part of the common Araneus genus.
Three-striped ground spider — small dark ground spider with three pale stripes. Fast nocturnal runner under debris. Ant-mimicking body shape.

fm.Color: Dark with 3 pale stripes; ant-like shape

desc: Asian flat-bodied jumping spider — tiny introduced salticid. Flat body adapted for living under bark and in crevices. On building exteriors. Originally from East Asia.
Worthy sheet weaver — tiny linyphiid spider building horizontal sheet webs in low vegetation. Common but overlooked due to minute size. Important predator of tiny insects.

fm.Color: Dark; tiny sheet web in low vegetation

desc: Lobed harvestman — small short-legged harvestman of moist canyon habitats. California native. Under rocks and in leaf litter. Unusual lobed body shape.
Icenoglei's tarantula — medium dark tarantula of foothill and mountain habitats. Nocturnal. Males wander in fall seeking females. California endemic.
Cockerell's harvestman — long-legged harvestman of scrub and woodland. Named for naturalist T.D.A. Cockerell. Scavenges on small invertebrates.
Oblong running spider — very elongated pale spider that stretches along grass blades and plant stems to hide. Extremely well camouflaged. Active hunter, no web.

fm.Color: Pale tan; very elongated; aligns with stems

desc: Belkin's short-tailed whipscorpion — tiny arachnid (schizomid) living in leaf litter and soil. Eyeless. Whip-like tail. Rarely seen — one of the most obscure LA arachnids.
Scalebroom bud mite — microscopic eriophyid mite inducing galls on scalebroom (Lepidospartum). Host-specific. Part of the enormous but invisible world of plant gall mites.
Long-spined meshweaver — tiny spider building irregular mesh webs in leaf litter. Thread-like web strands. Microscopic — requires close examination to find.
Multicolored longjawed spider — elongated spider with long chelicerae. Stretches along twigs when disturbed. Builds orb webs near water. Males interlock jaws during mating.

fm.Color: Variable tan/green; long jaws; near water

desc: Greedy trapdoor spider — large dark mygalomorph building silk-lined burrows with trapdoor. California endemic. Mountain and foothill habitats. Nocturnal ambush predator.
Rufous prodidomid — small reddish-brown ground spider. Fast nocturnal runner. Introduced from Mediterranean. Found under rocks and debris in gardens.
Classic ground spider — small pale spider with dark cardiac mark. Fast nocturnal hunter in leaf litter and under rocks. Part of the diverse ground spider family Gnaphosidae.
Bridge spider — large grey-brown orb weaver that favors man-made structures, especially bridges, buildings, and fences near lights that attract prey. Introduced from Europe.
Robust jumping spider — large flat salticid found under bark. Powerful jumper with excellent binocular vision. Hunts during the day. On tree trunks and fences.
Mountain jumping spider — small brown jumper of mountain and foothill habitats. Active day-hunter stalking prey on vegetation. Part of the diverse salticid fauna.
Bronze jumping spider — tiny metallic-bronze salticid. Active day-hunter on low vegetation. Males with iridescent chelicerae used in courtship displays.
California orbweaver — brown orb weaver named for Monterey County. Builds webs in garden shrubs and trees. Variable coloring. Nocturnal.
Mimicking long-jawed spider — medium orb weaver building webs in sheltered spots like caves, rock overhangs, and buildings. Related to Tetragnatha.

fm.Color: Brown; in sheltered webs — caves, overhangs

desc: Narrow ground spider — small elongated gnaphosid. Nocturnal runner in leaf litter. Dark with pale markings. Ant-like movement pattern may deter predators.
Lined orbweaver — slender orb weaver with elongated abdomen and fine longitudinal stripe. Builds webs in tall grass and reeds near water. Nocturnal.
Washona false widow — small dark theridiid building irregular tangle webs. Related to true widows but much less dangerous. Under eaves and in garages.
Obscure hairy scorpion — large pale scorpion of desert habitats. Nocturnal. Largest scorpion genus in North America. UV fluorescent. Moderate sting.
Persea mite — tiny spider mite causing silvering damage on avocado leaves. Builds webbing nests on leaf undersides. Important pest of California avocados.

fm.Color: Invisible; silvering damage on avocado leaves

desc: Dotted-leg cobweb spider — tiny theridiid with spotted legs. In vegetation and on buildings. Builds small irregular webs. Common but easily overlooked.
Two-spined orbweaver — small orb weaver with two small abdominal humps. In shrubs and low vegetation. Variable coloring. Part of the species-rich Araneus genus.
Southern coastal dune trapdoor spider — large mygalomorph of coastal dune habitats. Builds silk-lined burrow with trapdoor. California endemic. Threatened by coastal development.
Varroa mite — devastating ectoparasite of honey bees. Feeds on bee fat bodies. Primary driver of colony collapse disorder worldwide. Vectors bee viruses. Critical agricultural threat.

fm.Warning: Primary cause of honey bee colony losses

desc: Two-spotted spider mite — tiny red-green mite causing stippled yellowing on plants. One of the most important agricultural pests worldwide. Fine webbing diagnostic.
Western false widow — small dark theridiid. Builds irregular cobwebs under objects. Less common than S. grossa. Similar appearance to black widows but harmless.
Friendly paradise spider — tiny jumping spider with ornate male courtship involving colorful leg and palp displays. Ground-dwelling in open habitats.
Brown dog tick — the only tick that can complete its entire life cycle indoors. Primarily parasitizes dogs. Can transmit canine ehrlichiosis and babesiosis. Infests kennels and homes.
Zilla-like orbweaver — small brown orb weaver resembling European Zilla. Builds webs in garden shrubs. Nocturnal. Common but easily overlooked.
Grape erineum mite — microscopic eriophyid mite causing distinctive felt-like patches (erinea) on grape leaf undersides. Upper leaf surface shows blistered bumps. Common on backyard grapevines.
Red-and-white cobweb spider — small theridiid with distinctive red prosoma and white abdomen markings. In leaf litter and under debris. Less commonly seen than other theridiids.
Linsdale's meshweaver — tiny dictynid spider of California. Builds small mesh webs. Poorly known. Part of the cryptic ground-dwelling spider community.
Tent cobweb weaver — small theridiid building tent-like web. Males also self-amputate one palp. Related to T. haemorrhoidale.
Blue oat mite — tiny blue-black mite pest of winter cereals and pasture grasses. Important agricultural pest. Feeds on grass leaves causing silvering.
Tarsal paradise spider — tiny jumping spider with ornate male courtship. Males wave decorated leg segments in complex dances. Studied by behavioral biologists.

fm.Color: ♂ ornate legs for courtship dance; ♀ brown

desc: Marks' scorpion — medium scorpion of desert habitats. Nocturnal. UV fluorescent. Mild sting.
Same-colored jumping spider — small jumper with uniform coloring. Active day-hunter on vegetation.
San Bernardino Hills trapdoor spider — large mygalomorph of foothill habitats. Silk-lined burrow with trapdoor. California endemic. Restricted range.
Yellow cave harvestman — tiny pale yellow harvestman of caves and rocky crevices. Cave-adapted with reduced pigment. California endemic.
Eight-spotted jumping spider — medium jumper with eight pale spots. On desert scrub. Active day-hunter.

fm.Color: Dark with eight pale spots; desert

desc: Shore bdellid — tiny red predatory mite of coastal habitats. On rocks and beach debris. Predatory on other mites and small arthropods.
Tuberculated crab spider — small angular crab spider resembling a twig bump. Remarkable camouflage. Sits motionless on branches waiting for prey.
Heptagonal orbweaver — small orb weaver with angular heptagonal (7-sided) abdomen shape. Distinctive silhouette. In shrubs.
Cockroach cobweb spider — small theridiid with cockroach-brown coloring. In buildings and under debris. Builds irregular cobwebs.
Diplacis jumping spider — small brown jumper on native chaparral shrubs. Active day-hunter. Part of the diverse but difficult-to-identify Metaphidippus complex.
Chera jumping spider — small tan jumper of chaparral vegetation. Active day-hunter.
Sober plectreurid — medium spider of rocky habitats. California near-endemic family. In rock crevices.
California trachelid — small fast-running spider. Nocturnal. Under debris. Part of the poorly known Trachelidae.
Arizona black hole spider — large dark spider building messy webs in crevices and holes in buildings. Related to southern house spider. Harmless.

fm.Color: Dark; messy web in crevices; harmless

desc: Adanson's house jumper — small cosmopolitan jumping spider found in houses worldwide. Bold day-hunter on walls. Introduced from Old World tropics.

fm.Color: Dark with white spots; on indoor walls

desc: Enigma gall mite — microscopic eriophyid mite. Galls on native plants. Part of the diverse eriophyid fauna.
Shantz's tengellid — medium brown spider of foothill habitats. Fast runner. Part of the poorly known Tengellidae. California native.
Icenogle's trapdoor spider — trapdoor spider of foothill habitats. California endemic. Named for arachnologist Icenogle.
DiCaprio's crab spider — green crab spider NAMED FOR LEONARDO DICAPRIO in recognition of his environmental advocacy. On flowers and foliage. California.

fm.Note: Named for Leonardo DiCaprio — environmental honor

desc: Thompson's trapdoor spider — large dark mygalomorph building silk-lined burrows. California endemic. Nocturnal ambush predator.
Aiko's crab spider — small crab spider on flowers. Ambush predator. Can slowly change color to match flower.
Neo-Beevor's gall mite — microscopic eriophyid mite inducing galls on native plants. Part of the vast community of invisible plant-gall-forming mites that shape leaf and bud development.
Inland triangular cobweaver — small theridiid with triangular abdomen. In vegetation. Builds irregular web.
Five-plated whipscorpion — tiny arachnid (schizomid) in soil. Eyeless. Whip-like tail. Related to vinegaroons but microscopic. California endemic.

fm.Note: Schizomid — separate arachnid order; CA endemic

desc: Pirate otter spider — medium wolf spider of wetland margins. Runs on water surface to catch prey. Can dive underwater to escape predators.
Papenhoe's jumping spider — tiny colorful jumper on plants. Active day-hunter. Part of diverse western salticid fauna.
Barn funnel weaver — medium brown spider building sheet webs with funnel retreat in buildings, barns, and cellars. Harmless. One of the most common indoor spiders worldwide.

fm.Color: Brown with chevron marks; funnel web indoors

desc: Prodigal jumping spider — medium bold jumper. Like other Phidippus, curious and willing to investigate humans. Green chelicerae.
Gertsch's triangle spider — tiny spider building triangular web (only 4 radii). Holds web taut, vibrates it when prey hits. Unique web architecture.

fm.Note: Triangular web — vibrates to snare prey

desc: Hook-toothed money spider — tiny linyphiid spider. Ballooning dispersal — uses silk threads to fly on wind. Very common but microscopic.
Curly jumping spider — tiny jumper with crisp/curly markings. On vegetation. Active day-hunter. Named for the genus's connection to old English.
Berry mite — tiny bright red predatory mite. Extremely fast runner for its size. Important predator of pest mites and small insects on plants. Beneficial.
Ear mange mite — microscopic parasitic mite living in ear canals of dogs and cats. Causes intense itching and secondary infections. Common veterinary pest.
Scabies mite — microscopic burrowing mite causing scabies in humans. Tunnels under skin causing intense itching. Highly contagious. Important medical pest.
Avocado brown mite — tiny spider mite causing brown stippling on avocado leaves. Important pest of California avocados. Webbing on leaf undersides.
Hemorrhoidal cobweb spider — tiny theridiid. Males amputate one palp before mating — only spider known to do this. Bizarre reproductive biology.

fm.Note: Males amputate one palp before mating — unique

desc: Rose orbweaver — small green-brown orb weaver found on roses and other shrubs. Builds vertical orb web.
Scorched paradise spider — tiny jumping spider. Males with colorful courtship ornaments. Part of the most intensively studied courtship behavior genus.
Pajahuello tick — large soft tick found in deer bedding areas in mountains. Extremely painful bite — 'pajahuello' is Spanish for pest. Can cause allergic reactions.

fm.Warning: Extremely painful bite — allergic reactions possible

desc: Pale meshweaver — tiny pale dictynid building small mesh webs. On vegetation and buildings.
Ash flower gall mite — microscopic mite causing abnormal flower galls on ash trees. Flowers become swollen and persist as brown clusters.
Pomoa sac spider — small nocturnal hunting spider building silk retreats ('sacs') on foliage. Active night hunter.

fm.Color: Pale brown; silk retreat on leaves

desc: Citrus bud mite — microscopic mite infesting citrus buds causing distorted fruit ('lemon drop' fruit). Important citrus pest. Damages developing buds.
Surprising plectreurid — medium spider of rock crevices and caves. Part of the nearly California-endemic family Plectreuridae.
Lantana gall mite — microscopic mite causing flower galls on lantana. Introduced for biocontrol of invasive lantana.
Balia jumping spider — small striped salticid on vegetation. Active day-hunter with acute vision. Part of the well-studied Pelegrina genus. On shrubs in gardens and chaparral.
Avalon neon spider — tiny jumping spider from Catalina Island area. Among the smallest jumping spiders. Hunts in leaf litter.
Gosoga burrowing wolf spider — medium wolf spider living in permanent silk-lined burrow. Sits at entrance waiting for prey. Desert habitats.

fm.Color: Brown; in permanent burrow in desert

desc: Verity's running crab spider — small flat running spider on bark and walls. Very fast. Flattened body for bark crevices.

fm.Color: Brown; flat; very fast on bark

desc: Boxwood mite — tiny spider mite on boxwood. Causes leaf bronzing and drop. Pest of ornamental boxwood hedges.
Bearded ground spider — small dark gnaphosid. Fast nocturnal runner under rocks and debris.
Four-armed pseudoscorpion — tiny predatory arachnid with four-armed pincers (chelae). In leaf litter and soil. Hunts springtails and mites.
